June 22, 2026

Stucco Maintenance Tips for Austin Homeowners

One of the best things about stucco is how little maintenance it requires compared to wood siding or fiber cement that needs repainting every decade. But "low maintenance" does not mean "no maintenance," especially in Central Texas. Austin's combination of intense UV exposure, summer surface temperatures that routinely exceed 140 degrees on south- and west-facing walls, expansive clay soils, and seasonal storms means your stucco needs a little attention each year to deliver the fifty-plus-year lifespan it is designed for.

The good news is that the maintenance routine is simple, inexpensive, and something most homeowners can handle themselves. This guide covers what to do, when to do it, and when it is time to call a professional.

Why Austin Is Hard on Stucco

Before diving into the checklist, it helps to understand why Austin's climate demands a bit more vigilance than a milder region might.

  • **UV and heat** — Austin averages roughly 228 sunny days per year. That relentless sun degrades sealants, fades pigments, and drives daily thermal expansion and contraction that stresses the finish over time.
  • **Expansive clay soils** — Much of Travis County and surrounding areas sit on Blackland Prairie or Austin Chalk formations with highly expansive clay. When it rains the clay swells; during droughts it shrinks. That soil movement flexes foundations, and foundation movement is the leading cause of stucco cracking in Austin.
  • **Severe storms** — Hail, wind-driven rain, and occasional derechos can damage the finish and drive water into any crack or gap.
  • **Humidity swings** — Austin can go from bone-dry summer air to muggy 80-percent humidity in spring, creating conditions for mold and algae growth on shaded elevations.

None of these forces will defeat well-installed stucco on their own, but a small crack that goes unnoticed for a couple of seasons can let water into the wall — and water behind stucco is where real damage starts.

Annual Inspection Routine

Set a calendar reminder to walk your home's exterior once a year. Spring is ideal in Austin, after winter freezes have passed and before the brutal summer heat sets in. Here is what to look for on each elevation:

Cracks

Check around every window and door frame, at corners, along the roofline, and near any penetration like a hose bib, outlet, or AC line set. Hairline cracks are common and usually cosmetic, but anything wider than about one-sixteenth of an inch or cracks with staining nearby deserve a closer look. Pay extra attention to west- and south-facing walls, which take the most thermal stress.

Sealant and Caulk Joints

Inspect every caulked joint around windows, doors, vents, and where the stucco meets a different material. Sealant has a shorter lifespan than stucco itself and is the number-one entry point for water when it fails. Look for cracking, pulling away from the edge, or gaps. Replacing a five-dollar tube of sealant today can prevent a five-thousand-dollar moisture repair later.

Staining, Discoloration, and Algae

Water stains running down the wall, dark patches, or green algae growth all signal moisture issues. Stains below windows often indicate flashing problems. Algae and mold are common on north-facing walls shaded by trees, particularly in neighborhoods like Tarrytown and Hyde Park with mature live oak canopies.

Soft Spots and Hollow Sounds

Press on the stucco in a few spots on each wall. It should feel hard and solid. If an area feels soft, spongy, or sounds hollow when you tap it, moisture may be trapped behind the surface. This is especially important to check on EIFS (synthetic stucco) systems — see our guide to what EIFS is if you are unsure which system your home has.

Ground Contact and Drainage

Stucco should terminate several inches above grade and should never be in direct contact with soil, mulch, or standing water. Check that landscaping has not built up against the wall and that sprinklers are not hitting the stucco directly. In neighborhoods like Circle C Ranch and Avery Ranch where beds are often mulched right up to the foundation, this is worth checking every season.

Cleaning Your Stucco

Austin dust, pollen, and the occasional mildew buildup can dull your stucco's appearance. Cleaning once a year keeps it looking fresh.

What to Use

A garden hose with moderate pressure and a soft-bristle brush handles most surface dirt. For mildew or algae, mix one part white vinegar or oxygen bleach to four parts water and apply with a pump sprayer. Let it sit for ten to fifteen minutes, then scrub gently and rinse.

What to Avoid

  • **Pressure washers on high settings** — Excessive pressure can blast the finish coat right off, especially on older or EIFS-clad walls. If you use a pressure washer, keep it under 1,500 PSI and hold the nozzle at least twelve inches from the surface.
  • **Chlorine bleach** — It works on mildew but can discolor pigmented stucco and damage nearby plants. Oxygen bleach is safer.
  • **Wire brushes or abrasive pads** — These scratch the finish and expose the base coat underneath.

Seasonal Maintenance Calendar

Spreading tasks across the year keeps any single session short:

Spring (March to April)

  • Walk the exterior for cracks, stains, and sealant condition
  • Clean mildew or algae that built up over winter
  • Check that landscaping and mulch have not crept up against the stucco
  • Clear weep screeds and any drainage paths at the base of walls

Summer (June to August)

  • Re-inspect west- and south-facing walls midseason — these take the heaviest UV and thermal stress
  • Touch up any sealant that has cracked since spring
  • Keep sprinklers directed away from stucco surfaces

Fall (October to November)

  • Clean pollen and dust accumulation from the year
  • Trim tree branches that touch or shade the stucco and trap moisture
  • Check that gutters and downspouts are directing water away from stucco walls

Winter (December to February)

  • After any hard freeze, inspect for new cracks caused by freeze-thaw cycling
  • Check for ice dam conditions where a low roof meets a stucco wall

When to Call a Professional

Most of the maintenance above is homeowner-friendly, but some situations call for a pro:

  • **Cracks that keep coming back** after you have sealed them — recurring cracks usually mean the underlying cause was not addressed
  • **Soft spots, bubbling, or delamination** — these indicate moisture behind the surface and should be assessed before more water gets in
  • **Water stains on interior walls** near an exterior stucco wall — a clear sign that moisture has penetrated the system
  • **Large areas of discoloration or efflorescence** — persistent white deposits mean water is moving through the stucco
  • **Any damage to EIFS** — synthetic stucco requires manufacturer-matched materials and should not be patched with hardware-store products

For a deeper look at what stucco damage looks like and when it is urgent, see our guide to signs your Austin home needs stucco repair.

Protecting Your Investment

A few hours of attention each year is all it takes to keep stucco performing and looking great for decades. The return on that small investment is enormous — catching a hairline crack before it lets water behind the wall is the difference between a minor repair and a major remediation project.

If your annual inspection turns up anything that concerns you, Star Stucco of Austin offers free inspections across the Austin metro area. Explore our stucco repair services, learn about stucco crack repair, or contact us for a free assessment. As your local Austin stucco contractor, we are here to help you protect your home.

Frequently Asked Questions

How often should I inspect my stucco in Austin?

At least once a year, ideally in spring after winter freezes have passed and before the extreme summer heat. A second check on west- and south-facing walls midseason is also a good idea, since those elevations take the most UV and thermal stress in Austin's climate.

Can I pressure wash stucco?

Yes, but carefully. Keep the pressure under 1,500 PSI and hold the nozzle at least twelve inches from the surface. Excessive pressure can damage the finish coat, especially on older stucco or EIFS. A garden hose with a soft brush handles most cleaning without any risk.

What causes green or black stains on stucco in Austin?

Green stains are typically algae; black stains are usually mold or mildew. Both thrive on shaded, north-facing walls with limited air circulation, especially in Austin neighborhoods with mature tree canopies. Clean with a vinegar or oxygen bleach solution and trim any vegetation that traps moisture against the wall.

How long does stucco last in Austin with proper maintenance?

Properly installed and maintained stucco can last fifty years or more in Austin. The key is keeping sealant joints intact, addressing cracks before water gets behind the surface, and performing the simple annual inspection and cleaning routine outlined in this guide.
